summary: Simon Späti explains his migration from OneNote to Obsidian after 10+ years of note-taking. His core requirement was data ownership through plain-text Markdown files stored locally, rejecting proprietary formats that lock users in. After evaluating Notion, Roam, Joplin, and plain Markdown, he chose Obsidian because it's free, works as a wrapper around local folders and Markdown files, and offers features like Graph View. He provides Python scripts to help others export their OneNote notebooks to Markdown, demonstrating the technical process of escaping proprietary note-taking ecosystems.
key_points: Data ownership is paramount - notes should be plain-text Markdown files you control, not trapped in proprietary formats
Obsidian is free, works offline, and is essentially a beautiful wrapper around folders and Markdown files with one .obsidian config folder
Collaboration features are not essential for personal knowledge management - export to HTML/PDF when sharing is needed
Migrating from OneNote to Markdown is technically challenging but achievable with Python scripts and the Azure API
The flexibility of plain text means you can use any editor (vim, VS Code) or automate changes with scripts later
money_quotes: My goal was to own my notes as it's my data and my knowledge.
I didn't want to migrate from one proprietary format into the next one to lock me in yet another time.
Obsidian is just a wrapper around your folders and Markdown files, including one single folder called .obsidian in your root. That's it!
As everything is now plain text, I can search, replace or write a small Python script to fix these things later on.
In case you want to open all notes in one go, use open Obsidian.
key_insight: Own your notes by storing them as plain-text Markdown files locally - this gives you freedom from vendor lock-in and enables any future tooling or automation you might need.
